question: [File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can a pool sponsor (agreement owner) under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted stakeToken, expiry >= now+30d) call createPool with an accounts[] calldata array of length N (e.g. 5 000–50 000), causing _replaceScope to iterate N external isContractInScope calls and N _scopeAccounts.push SSTOREs, bypassing the existing guard (no accounts.length cap in createPool or _replaceScope), violating the invariant that pool creation must succeed for any valid scope subset, causing scoped impact: medium gas / block-gas-limit DoS on pool creation? Proof idea: Foundry fuzz test parameterising accounts.length from 1 to 50 000 with all accounts pre-seeded as in-scope on MockAgreement; assert gas consumed crosses block gas limit before accounts.length reaches a reasonable protocol maximum; assert createPool reverts with OOG at the threshold.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted token) call createPool with an accounts[] array where the last element is NOT in the agreement scope, causing _replaceScope to execute N-1 successful isContractInScope external calls and N-1 _scopeAccounts.push SSTOREs before reverting with AccountNotInAgreementScope, bypassing the existing guard (revert-on-invalid is intentional but no early-exit optimisation exists), violating the invariant that gas consumed before revert is bounded, causing scoped impact: medium gas waste / griefing of the sponsor's own transaction proportional to array position of the invalid account? Proof idea: Foundry unit test with accounts[0..N-2] valid and accounts[N-1] invalid; measure gas consumed vs. N; assert gas scales linearly with N and that a large N causes near-block-gas-limit consumption before the revert.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (valid agreement with N accounts in scope) call createPool with accounts[] of length N where accounts[N/2] is a duplicate of accounts[0], causing _replaceScope to execute N/2 isContractInScope external calls and N/2 SSTOREs before reverting with DuplicateAccount(accounts[0]), bypassing the existing guard (duplicate check uses isAccountInScope mapping set incrementally, not a pre-pass), violating the invariant that gas consumed before revert is bounded regardless of duplicate position, causing scoped impact: medium gas waste proportional to duplicate position in large arrays? Proof idea: Foundry fuzz test with accounts.length = 1 000, duplicate injected at fuzzed index; assert gas consumed scales with duplicate index and that placing the duplicate at index 999 wastes near-maximum gas.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted token, registry in NOT_DEPLOYED) call createPool with accounts[] of length N where all N accounts are valid, causing _replaceScope to make N external calls to IAgreement.isContractInScope and N _scopeAccounts.push operations, bypassing the existing guard (no per-call gas stipend or batch limit), violating the invariant that a single createPool call must fit within the block gas limit for any protocol-reasonable scope size, causing scoped impact: medium gas DoS — pool creation becomes impossible above a certain scope cardinality even with a fully valid scope? Proof idea: Foundry invariant test; for all accounts.length in [1, 10 000], assert createPool gas < block.gaslimit; find the maximum N at which creation still fits; document the threshold.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can an unprivileged attacker who controls a malicious IAgreement contract under preconditions (malicious agreement registered as valid in MockSafeHarborRegistry, attacker is agreement.owner()) call createPool, causing the malicious agreement's owner() to reenter createPool with the same agreement before _poolsByAgreement[agreement].push(pool) executes, bypassing the existing guard (no nonReentrant modifier on createPool), violating the invariant that the salt keccak256(abi.encode(agreement, _poolsByAgreement[agreement].length)) is unique per pool, causing scoped impact: medium — reentrant inner call reads the same pre-push length (0), computes the same salt, and Clones.cloneDeterministic reverts on address collision, causing the outer createPool to revert and leaving no pool created despite consuming full gas? Proof idea: Foundry unit test with a ReentrantAgreement mock whose owner() reenters createPool; assert outer call reverts; assert _poolsByAgreement[agreement].length == 0 after both calls; measure gas consumed.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can an unprivileged attacker who controls a malicious IAgreement contract under preconditions (malicious agreement registered as valid, attacker is agreement.owner(), a second valid agreement exists) call createPool with the malicious agreement, causing the malicious agreement's owner() to reenter createPool with the second valid agreement, bypassing the existing guard (no nonReentrant modifier on createPool, no cross-agreement reentrancy guard), violating the invariant that createPool is atomic and non-reentrant, causing scoped impact: medium — the reentrant inner call for the second agreement succeeds and pushes a pool to _poolsByAgreement[secondAgreement], then the outer call for the malicious agreement continues and also succeeds, resulting in two pools created in one transaction with interleaved state updates? Proof idea: Foundry unit test with ReentrantAgreement that reenters with a different agreement; assert both _poolsByAgreement arrays have length 1 after the outer call; assert no invariant on pool count is violated.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can an unprivileged attacker who controls a malicious IAgreement contract under preconditions (malicious agreement registered as valid, attacker is agreement.owner(), malicious isContractInScope reenters createPool) call createPool, causing the malicious agreement's isContractInScope (called from _replaceScope inside initialize) to reenter createPool with the same agreement, bypassing the existing guard (no nonReentrant modifier on createPool, reentrancy occurs deep inside initialize → _replaceScope → isContractInScope), violating the invariant that _poolsByAgreement[agreement].length is monotonically incremented once per createPool call, causing scoped impact: medium — reentrant call reads stale length (0), deploys a clone at the same deterministic address as the outer call's already-deployed clone, causing the reentrant Clones.cloneDeterministic to revert, which propagates up through isContractInScope → _replaceScope → initialize → createPool, reverting the outer call entirely? Proof idea: Foundry unit test with ReentrantAgreement whose isContractInScope reenters createPool; assert outer call reverts; assert no pool is registered.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can an unprivileged attacker who controls a malicious IAgreement contract under preconditions (malicious agreement registered as valid, attacker is agreement.owner(), malicious isContractInScope reenters createPool with a DIFFERENT agreement) call createPool, causing the malicious agreement's isContractInScope to reenter createPool with a second valid agreement, bypassing the existing guard (no nonReentrant modifier, reentrancy via deep call chain createPool → initialize → _replaceScope → isContractInScope), violating the invariant that createPool for agreement A does not modify _poolsByAgreement[B], causing scoped impact: medium — the reentrant inner call for agreement B succeeds and pushes a pool, then the outer call for agreement A continues and also succeeds, resulting in two pools created in one outer transaction with no reentrancy protection? Proof idea: Foundry unit test; assert _poolsByAgreement[B].length == 1 and _poolsByAgreement[A].length == 1 after the outer call; assert PoolCreated events emitted for both.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: getPoolsByAgreement] Can a pool sponsor (agreement owner) under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted token) call createPool N times for the same agreement, growing _poolsByAgreement[agreement] to length N, then cause any on-chain contract that calls getPoolsByAgreement(agreement) to OOG, bypassing the existing guard (no cap on pools per agreement, getPoolsByAgreement returns the full storage array as a memory copy), violating the invariant that pool enumeration must be callable by any on-chain consumer, causing scoped impact: medium gas / array-size DoS on pool enumeration — any integrator contract that calls getPoolsByAgreement in a transaction will OOG once N is large enough? Proof idea: Foundry fuzz test; create N pools for one agreement; call getPoolsByAgreement in a low-gas-limit context; assert the call reverts with OOG at a threshold N; document the maximum safe N.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: getPoolsByAgreement] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted token, no cap on createPool calls) call createPool repeatedly for the same agreement until _poolsByAgreement[agreement].length reaches N, causing getPoolsByAgreement to allocate a memory array of N addresses (N * 32 bytes) and copy N storage slots, bypassing the existing guard (no pagination, no length cap, no gas check), violating the invariant that view functions must be callable within reasonable gas bounds, causing scoped impact: medium — getPoolsByAgreement becomes uncallable on-chain (OOG) and returns an impractically large payload off-chain once N is large? Proof idea: Foundry invariant test; for all N in [1, 10 000], assert getPoolsByAgreement gas < block.gaslimit; find the N at which the view function first OOGs; assert poolCountByAgreement (O(1)) still works at that N.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: getPoolsByAgreement] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted token) call createPool N times for the same agreement, then cause a downstream contract that iterates over getPoolsByAgreement(agreement) to OOG, bypassing the existing guard (no per-agreement pool cap, no pagination interface), violating the invariant that factory enumeration functions must not enable DoS of downstream consumers, causing scoped impact: medium — any protocol integration that iterates the pool list on-chain is permanently DoS'd once the agreement accumulates enough pools? Proof idea: Foundry unit test with a PoolConsumer contract that calls getPoolsByAgreement and iterates the result; assert PoolConsumer.process() reverts with OOG after N pools; assert poolCountByAgreement still returns N correctly.

[File: src/ConfidencePool.sol] [Function: setPoolScope (triggered via ConfidencePoolFactory.sol createPool path)] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (pool created with old scope of N accounts, registry still in NOT_DEPLOYED or NEW_DEPLOYMENT so scopeLocked == false) call setPoolScope with a new accounts[] of M accounts, causing _replaceScope to first iterate N SSTOREs (isAccountInScope[old[i]] = false) then delete _scopeAccounts (O(N) storage clear) then iterate M external isContractInScope calls and M SSTOREs, bypassing the existing guard (no cap on old or new scope size), violating the invariant that scope replacement must complete within block gas limit for any valid scope sizes, causing scoped impact: medium gas DoS — scope replacement becomes impossible above a combined N+M threshold? Proof idea: Foundry fuzz test with old scope size N and new scope size M both fuzzed in [1, 5 000]; assert setPoolScope gas < block.gaslimit; find the (N, M) pair at which it first OOGs.

[File: src/ConfidencePool.sol] [Function: setPoolScope (triggered via ConfidencePoolFactory.sol createPool path)] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (pool created with old scope of N accounts, registry in NOT_DEPLOYED) call setPoolScope with a new accounts[] of M accounts where accounts[M-1] is NOT in the agreement scope, causing _replaceScope to clear all N old accounts (N SSTOREs to false, delete _scopeAccounts), then execute M-1 successful isContractInScope calls and M-1 SSTOREs before reverting with AccountNotInAgreementScope, bypassing the existing guard (EVM reverts all state changes, restoring old scope, but gas is consumed), violating the invariant that a failed scope replacement must not consume gas proportional to N+M-1, causing scoped impact: medium gas waste — a sponsor with a large existing scope can be griefed into wasting near-block-gas-limit gas on a failed replacement? Proof idea: Foundry unit test with N=1 000 old accounts and M=1 000 new accounts where new[999] is invalid; assert setPoolScope reverts; assert old scope is restored; measure gas consumed.

[File: src/ConfidencePool.sol] [Function: setPoolScope (triggered via ConfidencePoolFactory.sol createPool path)] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (pool created with old scope of N accounts, registry in NOT_DEPLOYED) call setPoolScope with a new accounts[] of M accounts where accounts[M/2] is a duplicate of accounts[0], causing _replaceScope to clear N old accounts then execute M/2 isContractInScope calls and M/2 SSTOREs before reverting with DuplicateAccount, bypassing the existing guard (duplicate check is incremental, not a pre-pass), violating the invariant that gas consumed before revert is bounded regardless of duplicate position, causing scoped impact: medium gas waste proportional to N + duplicate_position? Proof idea: Foundry fuzz test with N old accounts and M new accounts, duplicate at fuzzed index; assert setPoolScope reverts; assert gas consumed scales with N + duplicate_index.

[File: src/ConfidencePool.sol] [Function: setPoolScope (triggered via ConfidencePoolFactory.sol createPool path)] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (pool created with old scope of N accounts, registry in NOT_DEPLOYED) call setPoolScope with the same N accounts as the old scope (wholesale replacement with identical content), causing _replaceScope to clear N accounts (N SSTOREs to false, delete _scopeAccounts) then re-add N accounts (N isContractInScope calls, N SSTOREs to true, N push operations), bypassing the existing guard (no optimisation for no-op replacement), violating the invariant that a no-op scope replacement should not consume O(N) gas, causing scoped impact: medium gas waste — a sponsor can be forced to pay O(N) gas for a scope replacement that changes nothing? Proof idea: Foundry unit test with N=1 000 accounts; call setPoolScope with the same accounts; assert scope is unchanged; measure gas consumed vs. N.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (valid agreement whose isContractInScope has high gas cost, e.g. iterates a large on-chain list, allowlisted token) call createPool with accounts[] of length N, causing _replaceScope to make N external calls to IAgreement.isContractInScope each consuming G gas, bypassing the existing guard (no gas stipend limit on isContractInScope calls, no cap on N), violating the invariant that createPool gas cost is bounded by a protocol-reasonable constant, causing scoped impact: medium gas DoS — if the agreement's isContractInScope is expensive, even a small N can cause createPool to OOG? Proof idea: Foundry unit test with a MockAgreement whose isContractInScope burns a fixed amount of gas (e.g. 50 000 per call); assert createPool OOGs at N = block.gaslimit / 50 000; assert the threshold is below a reasonable scope size.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted token, accounts[] of length N) call createPool, causing _replaceScope to make N external calls to IAgreement.isContractInScope where each call's return value is not validated for gas forwarding (all available gas is forwarded), bypassing the existing guard (no gas cap per isContractInScope call), violating the invariant that the factory cannot be DoS'd by a high-gas-cost agreement, causing scoped impact: medium — a legitimate agreement with an expensive isContractInScope implementation makes pool creation prohibitively expensive or impossible? Proof idea: Foundry fuzz test with isContractInScope gas cost G fuzzed in [0, 100 000]; assert createPool succeeds for all G when N=1; assert createPool OOGs for G > block.gaslimit / N when N is large.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can a pool sponsor (agreement owner) under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted token, no cap on createPool calls per agreement) call createPool N times for the same agreement, growing _poolsByAgreement[agreement] to length N, bypassing the existing guard (no per-agreement pool count limit), violating the invariant that _poolsByAgreement[agreement] length is bounded, causing scoped impact: medium — the array grows without bound, making getPoolsByAgreement progressively more expensive and eventually uncallable on-chain? Proof idea: Foundry invariant test; assert _poolsByAgreement[agreement].length grows by 1 per createPool call; assert getPoolsByAgreement gas grows linearly with pool count; find the N at which getPoolsByAgreement first OOGs.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted token) call createPool N times for the same agreement, then cause poolCountByAgreement to return N correctly while getPoolsByAgreement OOGs, bypassing the existing guard (poolCountByAgreement is O(1) but getPoolsByAgreement is O(N)), violating the invariant that poolCountByAgreement and getPoolsByAgreement are consistent and both callable, causing scoped impact: medium — the two enumeration functions diverge in usability: count works but list OOGs, breaking any consumer that relies on the list? Proof idea: Foundry unit test; create N pools; assert poolCountByAgreement returns N; assert getPoolsByAgreement OOGs at the same N; assert the divergence is observable.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted token) call createPool twice for the same agreement in the same block, causing the salt keccak256(abi.encode(agreement, _poolsByAgreement[agreement].length)) to use length 0 for the first call and length 1 for the second call, bypassing the existing guard (salt incorporates per-agreement index), violating the invariant that two createPool calls for the same agreement produce distinct clone addresses, causing scoped impact: medium — if the length is not incremented between calls (e.g. due to reentrancy or a bug), both calls would compute the same salt and the second Clones.cloneDeterministic would revert? Proof idea: Foundry unit test; call createPool twice in the same block; assert two distinct pool addresses are returned; assert _poolsByAgreement[agreement].length == 2; assert salts differ.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted token, reentrancy via malicious agreement.owner()) cause the salt keccak256(abi.encode(agreement, _poolsByAgreement[agreement].length)) to be computed twice with the same length (0) before either push executes, bypassing the existing guard (no nonReentrant modifier, push happens after clone deployment and initialization), violating the invariant that each createPool call produces a unique clone address, causing scoped impact: medium — the reentrant inner call deploys a clone at the deterministic address, then the outer call tries to deploy at the same address and Clones.cloneDeterministic reverts, causing the outer createPool to revert and wasting all gas? Proof idea: Foundry unit test with ReentrantAgreement; assert outer createPool reverts; assert _poolsByAgreement[agreement].length == 0 after revert; assert no PoolCreated event emitted.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted token, accounts[] contains address(0)) call createPool with accounts[0] = address(0), causing _replaceScope to call IAgreement(agreement).isContractInScope(address(0)), bypassing the existing guard (no address(0) check in _replaceScope for scope accounts, only for constructor parameters), violating the invariant that scope accounts must be non-zero addresses, causing scoped impact: medium — if the agreement's isContractInScope returns true for address(0), a pool is created with address(0) in scope, which is semantically invalid and could cause downstream issues? Proof idea: Foundry unit test with MockAgreement.setContractInScope(address(0), true); call createPool with accounts[0] = address(0); assert pool is created; assert pool.isAccountInScope(address(0)) == true; assert this is an unintended state.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted token, accounts[] contains the pool's own address) call createPool where accounts[0] is the deterministically predicted pool address, causing _replaceScope to call IAgreement(agreement).isContractInScope(predictedPoolAddress), bypassing the existing guard (no self-reference check in _replaceScope), violating the invariant that scope accounts must be external BattleChain contracts, causing scoped impact: medium — if the agreement returns true for the pool's own address, the pool is created with itself in scope, which is semantically invalid? Proof idea: Foundry unit test; predict pool address via Clones.predictDeterministicAddress; set it in-scope on MockAgreement; call createPool with accounts[0] = predictedAddress; assert pool.isAccountInScope(pool) == true.

[File: src/ConfidencePoolFactory.sol] [Function: createPool] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (valid agreement, allowlisted token, accounts[] contains the factory's own address) call createPool where accounts[0] = address(factory), causing _replaceScope to call IAgreement(agreement).isContractInScope(address(factory)), bypassing the existing guard (no factory-address exclusion in _replaceScope), violating the invariant that scope accounts must be BattleChain contracts not protocol infrastructure, causing scoped impact: medium — if the agreement returns true for the factory address, the pool is created with the factory in scope, which is semantically invalid and could mislead stakers? Proof idea: Foundry unit test; set factory address in-scope on MockAgreement; call createPool with accounts[0] = address(factory); assert pool.isAccountInScope(address(factory)) == true.

[File: src/ConfidencePool.sol] [Function: getScopeAccounts (triggered via ConfidencePoolFactory.sol createPool path)] Can a pool sponsor under preconditions (pool created with accounts[] of length N, all valid) cause getScopeAccounts() to return a memory array of N addresses, bypassing the existing guard (no pagination, no length cap on _scopeAccounts), violating the invariant that view functions must be callable within reasonable gas bounds, causing scoped impact: medium — getScopeAccounts becomes uncallable on-chain (OOG) and returns an impractically large payload off-chain once N is large? Proof idea: Foundry fuzz test; create pool with N accounts; call getScopeAccounts; assert gas < block.gaslimit for N in [1, 10 000]; find the N at which getScopeAccounts first OOGs.
